dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to EMS for a possible stroke at a residence. Seventy-six-year-old male with left-sided weakness and altered mental status.
Hi sir, I'm with EMS. We're here to help you.
He was normal this morning and fed himself breakfast.
Tell me what happened this morning.
We left the room at 8:00 AM and he was at his baseline. We came back at 8:09 AM and found him with slurred speech and weakness on the left side.
So his last known well was 8:00 AM, and symptoms started around 8:09 AM?
Yes, that's correct.
His eyes are open spontaneously.
He is making incomprehensible sounds and is unable to speak coherently.
He follows commands but has significant weakness on the left side.
Glasgow Coma Scale is 12.
When was the last time he was normal?
He was at his baseline at 8:00 AM when we left the room.
We came back at 8:09 AM and found the slurred speech and left-sided weakness.
Does he have any history of stroke or other medical problems?
Yes. He had a stroke in April 2019 and has some residual right-sided deficits. He also has coronary artery disease, epilepsy, and colon, liver, and lung cancer.
Any allergies or blood thinners?
No known drug allergies. He takes [CITY].
Has he had any cough, sore throat, chest pain, trouble breathing, dizziness, nausea, vomiting, or abdominal pain?
No. He denies those. The main issues are altered mental status, unintelligible speech, and left-sided weakness.
Airway is patent.
Breathing is present and adequate with equal bilateral chest rise and fall. No respiratory distress noted.
Strong regular bilateral radial pulses are present.
Skin is pale, warm, and dry.
Pupils are 2 millimeters.
Blood pressure is 108 over 72.
Pulse is 60.
Respiratory rate is 16.
Blood glucose is 119.
Temperature is 98.2 degrees Fahrenheit.
Four-lead monitor is on.
Twelve-lead ECG shows sinus rhythm with PVCs and no significant ST abnormalities.
Family reports residual right-sided deficits from a prior stroke, but today's left-sided weakness and speech change are new.
No obvious trauma noted to the head or neck.
Patient has left-sided weakness and left-sided hemiplegia with altered mental status.
This presentation is consistent with stroke or TIA, and we are continuing the stroke protocol.
Patient is lifted from the recliner to the stair chair, strapped in, and safely taken outside.
Patient is then lifted from the stair chair to the stretcher, strapped in, and safely loaded into the ambulance.
[COMPANY_NAME] Emergency Department is contacted en route and a stroke alert is activated.
We're transporting emergently to [COMPANY_NAME] Emergency Department.
Patient is monitored throughout transport. Airway, breathing, and circulation remain stable.
Neurologic deficits remain unchanged during transport, with continued unintelligible speech and left-sided weakness.
Patient arrived at [COMPANY_NAME] and was taken directly to CT by stretcher. Care was transferred to the receiving RN after report, with transfer by sheet lift from EMS stretcher to CT bed.
